data.title: Alibaba Group Holding (NYSE:BABA) Margin Compression Tests Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
Advertisement

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) Q3 2026 earnings snapshot

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) just posted Q3 2026 results with revenue of ¥284.8b and basic EPS of ¥7.03, as investors weigh these numbers against a forecast calling for revenue growth of 9.1% per year and earnings growth of about 19.6% per year. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between ¥236.5b and ¥284.8b over the past six reported periods, while basic EPS has ranged from ¥5.36 to ¥21.07, set against trailing twelve month EPS of ¥40.04 and net income of ¥92.8b. With net profit margins over the last year below the prior year, this earnings print puts the focus squarely on how quickly profitability can track those growth expectations.

See our full analysis for Alibaba Group Holding.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the dominant market narratives to see which views are supported by the data and which might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Alibaba Group Holding

¥16.3b net income sits against 9.1% margin trend

  • Q3 2026 net income excluding extra items was ¥16.3b on revenue of ¥284.8b, compared with a trailing twelve month net income of ¥92.8b on ¥1,016.7b of revenue, which lines up with the 9.1% net profit margin referenced for the last year.
  • Consensus narrative assumes earnings of ¥171.1b by 2028 with margins easing from 14.8% to 13.6%. The latest trailing margin at 9.1% and trailing twelve month net income of ¥92.8b show current profitability sitting well below those future margin and earnings levels.
    • In that balanced view, analysts are effectively looking for earnings to rise from ¥148.3b today to ¥171.1b even as margins soften, while the trailing margin data highlight that recent profitability has been tighter than the 14.8% starting point in the narrative.
    • This contrast means anyone leaning on the consensus story needs to be comfortable that the gap between a 9.1% trailing margin and a 13.6% margin in three years can be bridged, even though current reinvestment into AI, cloud and quick commerce is weighing on EBITDA and free cash flow.

EPS swings from ¥21.07 peak to ¥7.03

  • Basic EPS has moved between ¥5.36 and ¥21.07 over the past six quarters, while trailing twelve month EPS is ¥40.04 compared with the five year EPS growth rate of about 0.4% per year that is cited in the analysis.
  • Bulls point to forecast earnings growth of roughly 19.6% per year and bullish scenarios that look for earnings to reach ¥219.7b with EPS of ¥91.35 by 2028. The recent EPS pattern, including Q3 2026 EPS of ¥7.03 and a trailing EPS of ¥40.04, underlines how much of that growth still sits ahead rather than in the historical track record.
    • The bullish view expects profit margins to climb from 12.4% to 15.1%, while the trailing net margin referenced at 9.1% and the modest 0.4% annual EPS growth over five years give a much more restrained picture of realised earnings progress so far.
    • This earnings gap means the bullish case leans heavily on future benefits from AI, cloud and quick commerce investments compared with what the last twelve months of EPS and margins actually show.
Consistent mid teens earnings growth and higher margins are central to the optimistic story, so it is worth seeing how that compares with the detailed bull case for Alibaba Group Holding 🐂 Alibaba Group Holding Bull Case.

DCF fair value and 196.78 target vs ¥124.9 price

  • The shares trade at ¥124.9, while the supplied DCF fair value is 249.17 and the allowed analyst price target figure is 196.78. The current price therefore sits well below both the DCF fair value and that target level.
  • Bears focus on the risk that heavy AI and quick commerce spending, including the referenced multi year RMB 380b AI and cloud CapEx plan and earlier comments on free cash flow turning into a large outflow, keeps margins and earnings under pressure. That would make it harder for the stock to move closer to either the 196.78 target or the 249.17 DCF fair value despite those apparent gaps.
    • The recent drop in trailing net profit margin from 12.3% to 9.1% and the negative recent year on year earnings movement highlighted in the analysis both align with the cautious view that profitability has been under strain during this investment phase.
    • At the same time, the P/E of 20.7x sits only slightly above the North American Multiline Retail industry average of 20.4x and just below the peer average of 21.4x, which bears may see as leaving limited room for valuation expansion if margins do not improve from current levels.
Skeptics argue that the current margin and cash flow profile could keep the share price closer to ¥124.9 than to modelled values, so it can help to review how the cautious case frames those risks in full 🐻 Alibaba Group Holding Bear Case

data.title: DoorDash Foot Locker Deal Expands Retail Delivery And Investor Questions
data.description:
• DoorDash (NasdaqGS:DASH) has launched a new nationwide retail partnership with Foot Locker, Kids Foot Locker, and Champs Sports.
  • The agreement brings nearly 1,300 athletic footwear and apparel locations onto DoorDash's on demand delivery marketplace.
  • The move extends DoorDash's offering beyond restaurant meals into same day delivery of shoes, clothing, and sports gear.

For DoorDash, best known for restaurant delivery, this partnership represents a deeper push into non food retail at a time when on demand apps are competing to handle more everyday purchases. Adding established apparel and footwear brands gives DoorDash another way to connect merchants with customers who expect quick delivery and broad selection. It also places the company more directly alongside other delivery platforms that already feature large retail partners.

For investors watching NasdaqGS:DASH, a central consideration is how expanded retail categories might affect order frequency, customer retention, and merchant relationships over time. If this type of partnership gains traction with both shoppers and brands, it may influence how DoorDash allocates capital, prioritizes product development, and positions itself within the wider retail and delivery market.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for DoorDash by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on DoorDash.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 4 things going right for DoorDash that every investor should see.

This partnership pushes DoorDash further into everyday retail, not just restaurant delivery, and plugs directly into an area where it already has traction in grocery and other retail categories. Foot Locker, Kids Foot Locker, and Champs Sports bring nearly 1,300 stores and a wide sneaker and apparel assortment into the app, which can help DoorDash increase use cases per customer and make DashPass membership more attractive. Limited time discounts on launch are likely aimed at accelerating adoption and testing demand patterns for same day shoe and apparel delivery. For you as an investor, the key question is whether these orders come on top of existing restaurant and grocery volumes, or simply replace some of them. The move also puts DoorDash in more direct comparison with delivery offerings from Uber, Instacart, and Amazon that already handle non food shopping. Executing well with large chains such as Foot Locker may strengthen DoorDash’s pitch to other retailers looking for on demand delivery without having to build their own logistics networks.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The DoorDash Narrative

  • The narrative highlights expansion into new verticals such as grocery and retail. This partnership fits that theme by increasing DoorDash’s role in local commerce beyond meals.
  • The narrative also warns that expanding into many regions and sectors raises complexity and execution risk. Coordinating thousands of apparel SKUs and return sensitive products could test DoorDash’s operations.
  • The impact of launch promotions, such as US$25 off US$100 orders, on long term order quality and customer behavior is not fully reflected in the narrative. These discounts can influence how quickly new verticals reach sustainable economics.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for DoorDash to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Deeper reliance on large retail chains increases DoorDash’s exposure to contract terms, margin pressure, and potential regulatory attention around partnerships, especially as policymakers review platform deals with big brands.
  • ⚠️ Managing footwear and apparel delivery, which often has higher return rates and size issues, could raise customer service costs or complicate unit economics if not priced carefully.
  • 🎁 Successful adoption across nearly 1,300 Foot Locker group stores could support higher order frequency per user, stronger DashPass value, and a broader merchant base tied into the platform.
  • 🎁 Retail partnerships of this type support the narrative of DoorDash as a general local commerce platform, which may help when competing for consumer spending against Uber, Instacart, Amazon and other delivery options.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how much apparel and footwear contribute to overall order mix, and whether management highlights Foot Locker, Kids Foot Locker, and Champs Sports as meaningful drivers of marketplace demand. Pay attention to commentary on profitability of non food verticals, DashPass engagement with retail orders, and any expansion of similar deals with other chains. Competitive responses from Uber and Instacart on same day apparel delivery could also signal how differentiated this move really is. Over time, the key question is whether partnerships like this help DoorDash build a more diversified, resilient stream of orders that can support the broader investment case.

data.description:

Preferred P/E of 14.1x: Is it justified?

CVR Partners is trading on a P/E of 14.1x at a last close of $131.26, which screens as inexpensive compared with both the US Chemicals industry average and its closest peers.

The P/E ratio compares the company’s share price to its earnings per unit, so a lower figure often indicates the market is paying less for each dollar of current earnings.

Here, the 14.1x P/E sits well below the US Chemicals industry average of 27.1x and the peer average of 28.2x. The company has high quality earnings and has moved into profitability over the past five years, with earnings growth of 11.2% per year over that period and 62% earnings growth in the last year, alongside a net profit margin of 16.3% compared with 11.6% a year earlier.

That gap suggests the market is currently valuing CVR Partners’ earnings at a clear discount to sector and peer pricing, even though recent earnings growth exceeds the Chemicals industry result of a 2.5% decline over the same period.
